Pat, one of your peers who leads another BU, has been managing a tricky situation with one of Transformation Inc.'s biggest customers: Big Box. They leverage solutions across the portfolio, but Pat's BU provides the most solutions to Big Box. Recently, Big Box has become increasingly demanding.

To address the challenge, Pat has called together a "Response Team" comprised of leaders across multiple BUs affected by Big Box's demands, which includes your BU. In the first Response Team meeting, you learn Big Box is demanding new requirements to be met before committing to our solutions for the upcoming year. A few team members raise the concern that these demands could lead Transformation Inc. in a different direction than the current priorities and will be time-consuming for many of your peers to accommodate.

You sense frustration from the Response Team around what to do. Some of your cross-functional peers suggest raising prices to accommodate the additional work, while some are questioning whether it makes sense to accommodate the requests at all, which means risking losing Big Box as a customer.

You recently had a challenging experience with a customer where your company struggled to meet their needs. To increase your team's customer centricity, you decide to bring the insights from the customer challenge to a team offsite.

At the offsite, you share the customer story paired with some new and relevant market trends research. You challenge the team to generate more customer-focused ideas for our solutions and our processes.

In response, Neo, one of your team members comes up with a disruptive idea that surprises you. You've seen a similar idea implemented at Revolution Inc. a few years back but due to lack of organizational support, it failed. However, if implemented successfully, this idea could potentially drive significant new revenue, though in another business unit. It could also require a lot of coordination with several other parts of the company, shifting priorities. Your own team is at capacity with ongoing priorities and this idea could exceed the team's scope of responsibility.

The whole team is watching for your response.

A week after the team offsite, you are meeting with the senior leadership team. Although not fully bought in, you share the disruptive customer-first idea. Many of your peers on the leadership team agree it would disrupt more than our customer approach; it would require us to rethink parts of our strategy and change our ways of working.

Surprisingly, Maria, your boss, says she would like to hear more about what this could look like in action. You explain the idea and share the reasons it would be challenging to pursue, given other priorities. A few days later during a 1:1, Maria decides this is an area we need to explore. She suggests making it a customer-first initiative and ask you to lead the efforts.

"I'd like to see what you can make of this," Maria says. "Please come prepared with an update on your progress to present to the leadership team during our next meeting in two months."

During the past few weeks, there has been significant progress with the new customer-first initiative. You also notice that the initiative has brought prioritization and accountability challenges to your team. You have a plan to address these shifts with a new approach to some key roles in across Transformation Inc.

As you begin to communicate role changes with your peers on the leadership team, you discover that getting approvals for the new roles has been harder than you expected. Another week passes and you are still not able to announce the changes in roles and responsibilities.

To make matters worse, you learn that the other BU leaders did not expect the changes you proposed and believe it may cause challenges within their BUs which they don't have the bandwidth to address. Confusion and frustration are increasing throughout the organization and excitement for the initiative is dying down as ways of working are unable to keep pace with the changes. You decide to call sync with impacted leaders from your BU to explore the impact of the delays and share the future plan for the initiative.

It's been two months since you were asked to lead the efforts on a new customer-focused initiative, and you are ready to present your insights to the leadership team. At the meeting, you share some early positive customer feedback along with concerns that your continued focus on the initiative will cause your core business to suffer, especially as your teams are struggling with the various changes.

To your surprise, the leadership team is thrilled by the initial results with customers. As a result, they want to turn the initiative into a stand-alone business venture in support Transformation Inc.'s greater strategy. Your work on the initiative so far will be the foundation for the new venture, which will now become a significant part of your existing business. You must now decide how to best balance your obligations to the legacy business while supporting the launch of the new business.

The new venture is almost ready to launch, and you have been transparent about the big changes it will bring to your business unit. You notice a lot of different reactions to the changes from your people across your organization. Some are fully engaged and aligned, while others are doubtful and unsure about the implications the changes will have on their jobs.

In response to this feedback, you begin a reorganization to create new permanent teams, which unfortunately means you will break up teams who have worked well together.

As you begin to socialize the re-organization, the teams working on the legacy business feel demotivated because they feel their work is harder yet the value is increasingly unclear. At the same time, the teams transitioned to the new business feel uncertain about what they are supposed to do to succeed in this new role.

A. Hold steady and protect delivery: Stay focused on current commitments and use the new tool only where expectations are already clear. Acknowledge the mixed messages to the team, but signal that quality and consistency come first while things stabilize. You'll bring clearer guidance to the team as soon as it's available.

B. Move fast and learn as you go: Encourage the team to start using the tool broadly across their work. Set up a quick rhythm to share what's working and adjust together in real time. The priority is building momentum and learning through doing, even if the approach isn't fully consistent yet.

C. Slow down to speed up: Before deciding how to move forward, bring the team together to name what's clear and what isn't. Use the conversation to identify where the team most needs to adapt and focus energy there first, while keeping everything else steady.

D. Discuss the Impact: Explain to the Response Team how these requests will impact your BU and ask for their perspectives on how the request will impact their BUs as well. As you listen to your peers, you question whether these changes are possible for the teams to implement so quickly. Let Pat know that you do not feel we are moving towards a solution that is in line with Transformation Inc. current priorities and that it would be unsustainable for the company to rearrange priorities for every large customer.

The team feels stability and relief - there's less noise, and current work stays on track. But by signaling that the team waits for clarity before adapting, you've unintentionally made the team more dependent on top-down direction. The gap between current habits and future expectations widens and by the time clearer direction arrives, the team has more to unlearn than if they had started adapting earlier.

Energy picks up and the team starts building real familiarity with the tool. Some genuine learning happens that wouldn't have come from waiting. But without a shared sense of where to focus first, people apply the tool differently — outputs become inconsistent, rework creeps in, and teammates who needed more structure before moving start to disengage.

By getting specific about what's known and unknown before acting, the team moves with intention rather than waiting or scrambling. Shared clarity replaces competing interpretations, and the team builds confidence in navigating uncertainty together. But this only works if the conversation goes deep enough to produce a real next step — if it stays too high-level, the team walks away with a framework but not a clear direction.

Sharing your perspective helped the response team slow down to consider the impact on each BU to meet the requirements. However, some of your peers who were bought in to supporting Big Box were able to bring their teams along, while you needed more time, which caused misalignment across the company. Big Box also soon becomes frustrated with the Transformation Inc.'s inability to meet their needs and decide to look for another solution who can keep up with new market demands. As it turns out, they were looking for a more holistic solution. Your desire to keep the current priorities intact rather than spend time exploring the deeper customer need caused you to miss an opportunity to develop a stronger relationship with this customer and your cross-functional peers.

A. Tighten the approach before trying again: Acknowledge the friction but then redirect the team toward fixing the process. Clarify expectations for what gets shared with the other team and when, so the next handoff is better set up. Keep the conversation focused on what's actionable and move forward.

B. Step back before acting: Before deciding what to do next, ask the team to share what each person actually saw and experienced — including what it may have felt like from the other team's side. Use that shared understanding to decide how to move forward together.

C. Refocus on the goal and commit to trying again: Bring the team back to why this pilot matters and what it's trying to achieve. Acknowledge that the first attempt was rough, but keep the conversation focused on what to do differently next time rather than dwelling on what went wrong. Agree on one small adjustment and move forward quickly.

D. Consult with Leadership: Thank Neo for sharing the idea and make sure they understand how valuable this type of ideation is for your BU to continue to grow. Explain that, while it's a bold and interesting idea, given capacity constraints and pressing priorities elsewhere, you're not sure if we can implement it without senior leadership's approval. Tell them you will share the idea at the next leadership team meeting and get back to them with their thoughts.

The team leaves with clearer expectations and a more structured plan for the next handoff. Things feel more organized and the other team gets a response. But because the conversation stayed focused on process, the differing interpretations of what happened never got addressed and the tension between teammates quietly carries forward into the next attempt.

By creating space for each person to share what they actually saw and experienced, the team starts to understand why they've been talking past each other. What felt like disagreement turns out to be people reacting to different parts of the same situation. The team lands on a path forward that everyone has genuinely contributed to, which makes them more likely to follow through on it together.

The team regains momentum and a sense of shared purpose. The adjustment feels manageable and the conversation ends on a constructive note. But by moving quickly past what happened, the underlying tensions between teammates don't get resolved and the differing interpretations that created friction in the first place are still there, waiting to show up again in the next handoff.

Neo thanks you and commits to following up with you after the leadership meeting. Excitement begins to fade in the room as your team members begin to question what we can do now to be more customer-focused, or whether we always must wait for senior leadership's approval. They don't say it, but the team begins to question what decisions you have the authority to make.

A. Ask for Support: You appreciate Maria's commitment to customer centricity but are feeling a little overwhelmed adding this to your plate. Ask Maria for guardrails regarding timing, budget, and other resources in turning this high-level idea into a detailed customer-first initiative. You want to confirm that you have their support and plan to touch base with Maria throughout the month to keep the momentum going.

B. Invite Input: You agree to lead the next stage of the effort, despite reservations. Recap the points for and against the idea as you heard them in discussions thus far and identify the areas your team members and senior leaders agree and disagree on. Schedule follow-ups with the dissenters from your team and the senior leadership team before your team starts putting the plan together to create greater buy-in. You want to understand their concerns and find a way forward that you can agree on.

C. Make it your Own: Thank Maria for the opportunity. Let her know that in order for the initiative to be successful, you want to make it your own, and address some of the challenges you may face. Think through the dissenters from the leadership team meeting so you can meet with them 1:1 to understand and discuss their concerns. Using the data from these 1:1 discussions, you tweak the idea in a way you are more aligned with leading to share with Maria, and take the opportunity to delegate tasks that will support the effort.

D. Explore the Idea First: Thank Maria for the opportunity but let her know you are not ready to commit yet. You agree this is an interesting area to explore, especially after the feedback you've heard from the rest of the leadership team but emphasize that this new idea may diverge from Transformation Inc.'s current priorities and adversely affect other business units. Remind Maria about a similar initiative that went poorly. Tell her you'll brainstorm and come back with suggestions on ways to increase customer centricity that are aligned with the company's current priorities.

Maria appreciates your positive attitude in taking on this new initiative. However, you realize that she is also uncertain on what the best direction is and asks you to take ownership to propose a plan in your next 1:1. Maria's response makes you increasingly uncertain about how to continue with this customer-first initiative, only adding to your exhaustion from continuous change in the business. You leave the meeting worried about the implications the change in direction could cause.

By recapping the pros and cons, you find out that Maria also has doubts but still feels this is something worth exploring. She encourages you to take it forward with an open mind. During your follow-up conversations with the dissenters, you gain insights around implications you hadn't considered, helping you identify useful next steps. Your peers feel heard, and some of them offer to help the exploration process as they now trust that you listen to both perspectives and are aware of how the changes will affect them.

As you move through your meetings with dissenters, most leaders seem to agree with you and validate your doubts. However, after a couple of weeks, only a few of your peers have completed the tasks you've delegated. You follow up with them, but they explain they have other pressing priorities. Some say they don't see how the tweaked idea would have the same potential value as the original customer-first idea; they feel the changes you made for expediency took the strength out of the customer-first initiative. Moreover, Maria pushes back; she's not sure how the tweaked idea would have the same potential value as the original customer-first idea. She asks you to rethink your approach.

Maria appreciates you sharing your perspective and your laser-focus on the company's current priorities, but still insists that you further explore the customer-first initiative. Your boss shares that she completely trust that you can carry this forward; her their door is open for any questions or ideas as you get started. You appreciate the trust and autonomy but are still not bought-in to the idea, which slows your momentum to lead this initiative.

A. Share your Plan: While the changing of roles across the organization has been slow, it is only one step in implementing the initiative. You want to maintain momentum, so you decide to share a milestone plan in the meeting that details the future changes for the organization. You let your team know that you want this to be done right and that you will schedule recurring meetings to hold other leaders accountable for progress on the customer-first initiative as well. You know this may require making unilateral decisions that people may not like but will be necessary to accelerate changes within the company.

B. Pivot your Approach: In the meeting, you decide to candidly lay out the issues that have been delaying the initiative and ask for the team's patience as you address some of these challenges. Since the current plan is not going smoothly, engage with your direct reports to identify temporary changes that can be made without redefining roles or implementing broad company-wide changes. You also plan to connect with influential decision-makers separately to gain support for these alternate ideas first, before bringing it to the leadership team.

C. Invite your Boss: Invite your boss Maria to the meeting to provide a unified front. Your teams need to hear the vision for the customer-first initiative from the top to remind them not to be discouraged by the confusion over roles. Remind your teams that change takes time and that while there won't be role clarity across the organization right away, you acknowledge the frustration it has created. Recognize individuals who are taking first steps for the initiative and echo your boss's vision.

D. Drive Focus: You decide to use this meeting as an opportunity to share your view on what has been decided and is in motion, rather than focusing on what is still outstanding. Admit that gaining alignment and approvals, especially for the new role descriptions, has been more difficult than you anticipated, and take responsibility for the delay. Listen to questions and frustrations aired by the organization. You acknowledge that change is hard and encourage focusing on what teams control and can start executing on now.

Your teams keep pushing through, but as confusion and complications increase due to the lack of clarity on their roles, they start to lose faith in the potential of the new initiative despite the detailed milestone plan. More and more individuals complain, and you notice that you are increasingly falling behind on other key milestones as well. You try to encourage teams to get them back on track, but it's hard to spark their enthusiasm when they are unaligned or unclear on decisions being made.

As your direct reports wait to hear back from you on these alternate ideas, they are uncertain on how to move forward with their teams. They decide to let their teams continue with "business as usual" until they hear more. While your direct reports are getting anxious and discouraged with how to proceed, you let them know you are working to get your peers on board with a more realistic plan. In the meantime, you find differing perspectives from some of your discussions with key decision-maker, which further slows you down.

This approach ensured that the vision for the initiative landed correctly, and a few team members share their appreciation over having the opportunity to hear from your boss directly. While the team felt encouraged by Maria's optimism for the initiative, you are not sure how long this will last without making necessary changes for the roles. This approach also placed some doubt in your ability to lead the initiative and drive clarity behind the need for change to the organization without needing to go to the top.

Being transparent about the current state, reassuring your teams about the future, and acknowledging your responsibility for the delay were great ways to show empathy for your organization in the midst of change. While some of the questions raised in the meeting were challenging, the open communication drove a culture of transparency and your teams felt more encouraged. Though several individuals are still troubled by the challenges of continuing to work like this, they are more open adapting as they go to speed up the approval process. Following the meeting, a few team members reach out to volunteer taking responsibility to move the initiative along.

A. Secure Support: Create a team that will lead this new part of the business. Given senior leadership's support, you feel confident in this new venture and look forward to what it will allow the company to achieve. Engage a subset of the leadership team to act as a steering committee for this new venture team, especially during the initial stages. You hope that having continued support and access to the leadership team will allow you to stay focused on the legacy business while your new venture team works on the new business.

B. Minimize Disruption: Identify a few people on your team who can begin to plan the new venture launch to minimize disruption to the existing business. Create a team with some of the most senior people in your business unit who will have full authority to oversee the implementation. You will be involved only when needed to allow you to meet your current obligations to the existing business. Have the team identify options for implementation and conduct a thorough risk analysis of each option, since the responsibility for success will still rest with you.

C. Act Quickly: There is no time to waste — you assign your best people to lead the new venture. You run consistent stand-up meetings with the new venture team to keep track of what's going on and create a forum for the team to ask questions. To make it workable, you have them begin to transfer their legacy work immediately to ensure a clean separation. Continue running the legacy business at the same time, mostly through weekly staff meetings.

D. Ramp up the Team: Dedicate significant time upfront to prepare your team who will lead the execution of the new venture and explain the vision. At the same time, you work to reconfigure your remaining team to run the existing business. Stay close to both efforts and have one-to-ones with some of your direct reports to learn what's working and what's not. Do your best to remove obstacles for other teams across the company involved in the new venture and prepare for any risks to the venture's success. You know that the necessary changes to the legacy business have only just started.

The team members leading the new venture are excited about the visibility of their new roles, but as they execute on the venture, they struggle to manage competing priorities from senior stakeholders. With only limited guidance and involvement from you, the team was unable to balance the short and long-term objectives for the new venture. Although they turned to the steering committee, the senior leaders did not have clarity into your business's current priorities and were not always able to lead your team in the right direction.

The new team working on the new business started off strong. By delegating and providing autonomy, they were able to achieve many of the goals you identified. However, down the road you noticed that certain BU needs were deprioritized by the new venture team, which you believe might create issues for the legacy business. As you dig deeper to understand why, you realize the team was focused on the short-term priorities and were unclear on the bigger picture. Before having the team dive into the new venture, you missed an opportunity to work with them to define a more balanced approach.

Through taking immediate action, you were able to make progress on the new venture fairly quickly. Focusing your time and energy on getting the new business started also created engagement and clarity among the team you assigned to do the work. However, after some time, one of your team members comes to you and reports a decline in both sales and profitability within your legacy business. Some of the team members you've assigned to the new venture were crucial drivers of the success of your legacy business. Moreover, your teams running the legacy business have lost clarity and engagement due to the high focus on the new venture.

It took longer than expected for the legacy and new business teams to clearly understand the objectives of the new venture and how to work together at first in this new structure. But as time passes, the team begins to take ownership of their respective parts as you stay close to both efforts. You even get suggestions from team members within both the new venture and legacy business on what needs to be considered to successfully integrate the new venture into the way we work. At the same time, the legacy business continues to run smoothly, providing a stable base for you to be able to explore the new venture thoroughly.

A. Surface Doubts: Meet with your direct reports to better understand their concerns and doubts. Take the time to validate their concerns by expressing your own doubts. Reassure them that we are already over the biggest hurdle with this change and that launching the venture will not be much different from where they are now. Reiterate the importance of both the legacy and new business to the company. Additionally, demonstrate you understand the level of change they've committed to and that you recognize and appreciate their efforts.

B. Discuss the Risks: While it wasn't part of your plan, bring the new venture team and the legacy team together to share their plans and identify implementation risks together. Although they may have conflicting perspectives, invite candid debates and drive a conversation about the impact on our customers. Highlight the importance of both teams' work to our customer-focused strategy without downplaying the seriousness of the challenges and point to potential solutions each team has already considered.

C. Bring in Leadership: Now is a great time to engage some of your peers and the senior leadership team to help the venture get through the launch. Ask these leaders to send a message to your teams about the importance of both the legacy business and the new venture to highlight the value of their work and how it ties to the larger strategy. You trust some of your peers will offer an open door to discuss any questions or concerns your team members may have. Your leadership team peers can add more background to what we are aiming to accomplish and the benefits to our customers.

D. Strategic Communication: Call a BU meeting with the legacy and new business to answer questions and help relieve some anxiety for the change. You want your teams to feel confident in the importance of both parts of the business to the overall success of the company and take pride in the impact both will have on our customers. Work with the communications team to help you share your vision in the most compelling way. To be fully prepared, ask for questions in advance. It's show time!

Meeting with your direct reports gives you a better understanding of their general concerns, but without a clear purpose to rally around, your teams still feel uncertain about their future at the company. They follow through with what they need to do but are not enthusiastic about their work, and sharing your own doubts only adds to their concerns. You ultimately missed an opportunity to ground both the legacy business and the new customer-first venture to our customers' needs to show the value of your people's work.

Bringing both the legacy and new venture teams to work together and identify risks to the company and customer creates trust.  You've empowered them to work out the implications of the new venture on their work themselves. The new venture team was able to identify flaws in their current thinking from the legacy business, which may have had larger implications for the success of the venture down the road. A couple of team members from the legacy team also approach you after the offsite and thank you for listening to them, sharing that they were skeptical at first but now can see potential benefits and opportunities from the new venture. Aligning the teams' work around the customer need was an effective way to re-engage your teams. Time well invested!

The senior leadership team and your peers were able to add more clarity for your teams, but the vision for the customer-first venture gets lost in all the different conversations. Without anchoring this venture to the customer-first need and showing the value of both the legacy and new businesses, your teams have trouble seeing the value in their work.

Your planning with the communications team paid off as you don't encounter many challenging questions during the meeting. It seems to you that both the legacy team and new venture team are on board with your vision. However, later that week one of your peers notes that productivity in several teams seems to have dropped and that they have big doubts about the new venture. You are unsure about what to do next, as the BU meeting didn't give you the result you were hoping to achieve. You ultimately missed an opportunity to ground both the legacy business and the new customer-first venture to our customers' needs to show the value of your people's work.
What are your key takeaways from this session?

In addition, business is getting impacted by the crisis with a record high cancellation rate. Your employees' productivity is now as low as 40%.

"What is your reaction to what I'm saying?"

High Quality Advocacy

The new business venture is growing steadily, with more teams involved in supporting and developing its efforts. During a weekly meeting with one of your teams, they describe a challenge they are having with getting the commercial sales team to market the new offering. The current compensation plan for sales representatives promotes the legacy offerings over the new offerings.

You draft a recommendation for addressing this issue and meet with the compensation and commercial leads to present your thinking. Karim, the compensation lead, is skeptical, referring to the long and successful tenure of the existing structure. Further, Karim emphasizes an HR committee would need to make the approval for the change. Grace, the commercial lead, on the other hand, is more supportive, describing in detail her suggested next steps.

After hearing them both, you see merit in their concerns and ideas, but you have a different view on the best approach. The three of you leave the meeting with three divergent perspectives.

A. Alignment Meeting: You may need to involve members of the Senior Leadership Team to find common ground. Set up a meeting with relevant members of the SLT, Karim and Grace, during which all three of you will have the space to share your perspectives and then the SLT will make the final recommendation. In advance of the meeting, each of you agrees to follow through on the SLT's recommendation. Ground the meeting in the importance of aligning on new incentives, as this will be the groundwork for the success of the new ventures' offering.

B. Find a Compromise: You worry that Karim's resistance may cause the new compensation structure to fail. Bring the leads together to discuss the needs and find a compromise, expressing the importance of new incentives for the new venture to succeed. Small steps are enough to move forward, and although it will take more time, you hope a compromise will generate alignment on the changes.

C. Get to the Root Cause: Schedule more time with the compensation and commercial leads and ask probing questions to better understand the root cause of the compensation lead's skepticism, and the rationale behind the commercial lead's suggested next steps. Using their answers, recap both the pros and cons linked to changing the structure and identify a set of alternatives that could be pursued. With these ideas in mind, ask them to join you in a working session with the purpose of getting to a viable solution quickly that will allow the team to capitalize on the opportunities within the new venture, while mitigating the risks.

D. Make Updates: It's clear that you haven't done enough groundwork to be able to convince your peers. Go back and update your suggestion, developing stronger arguments for why it's the best way forward and adding comments from other leaders in the organization supporting your cause. Then schedule separate meetings with the commercial lead and compensation lead to discuss further.

Each of you honors your agreement to follow through on the SLT's recommendation. However, their decision was not aligned with any of your original perspectives. Without fundamentally aligning behind the changes ahead of approaching the SLT, it took longer than anticipated to implement the suggested changes and no one felt satisfied with the outcome.

While compromise takes time, each of you felt heard and were able to understand different parts of why there was a need for change. You were able to come to a compromise; however, when it came time to explain the reason behind the final changes being proposed, none of you were able to fully articulate the reasons. Although it's great to compromise, without a common goal or co-created a solution, the changes felt arbitrary, which raised more questions than it solved.

Engaging the compensation lead and commercial lead in co-creating a solution while reminding them of the overall purpose and opportunities proves to be successful. As you listen to their concerns, you build trust while increasing your own understanding of what might be a viable way forward for everyone involved. When you asked them to contribute with their expertise and help find a solution, it resulted in a decision they both own and are proud of.

In your next meeting, you manage to convince the commercial lead to support your plan. You are less fortunate with the compensation lead, who continues to defend the current structure, referring to the percentage of overall turnover that still comes from the legacy business. Despite your hard work, your approach took too long to get the new offering out in time. The compensation lead also seems to have lost trust in you and is reluctant to set aside time to continue discussions, referring to other priorities. You missed an opportunity to collaborate on a way forward by holding on too tightly to your own perspective.

With the incentive structure now supporting the new venture and teams getting ready to launch, you sense some new confusion about the relationship between your business unit's strategy and the new venture's goals. After speaking with some team members to understand what people are confused about, you identify a gap in performance expectations and their goals.

Though you're hesitant to introduce more change, you recognize you need to update the business unit's performance expectations in the upcoming planning cycle. Some of your team members played a big role in creating the current plan and you bring a suggested change to performance expectations to them first.

Their response was resoundingly negative. Most of your team feels there is no need to change the current goals or expectations.

A. Lessons Learned: Given how much the business has changed since the last planning cycle, you see the new performance goals as a way for your team to demonstrate what they have learned and to set very tangible targets. Since some of the team members are resisting setting new goals, you put a temporary hold on the planning. You begin to look back and document what the team has learned and tried so far. Feeling proud of where you are now, you take this document and attitude to the team as a conversation starter for updating the performance goals to better reflect their current work.

B. Take a Step Back: Emotions are high and as you listen to the pushback you feel that some people are overly concerned about the "what-ifs" as it relates to the new performance goals. You let them know that you will consider all their input before making a final decision and remind them of the whole team's success to date. Based on what you know about the team's current success and the work they are doing, you feel there is nothing to worry about and take a step back on updating the performance goals.

C. Consult your Peers: The pushback makes you realize there are many things you had not considered. The team members you talked to are closer to the work and don't feel a rewrite is needed while those who are not as close to it feel their work is not being recognized. You consult with your BU peers to see if anyone else is facing similar issues. You learn that some of your peers decided to wait and take this conversation case by case, while others immediately updated their team's performance goals. You decide to wait and deal with each case individually.

D. Make the Changes: You have learned a lot since the last planning cycle and have several ideas on how to make these changes to the performance goals for the new venture. While the pushback from the team is substantial, you know that the right thing to do is to move forward with the necessary changes. You know it is up to you to make it happen, so you brace yourself for any pushback as you proceed to make the necessary changes.

Starting with looking back at what the teams have accomplished gave the team a similar level of pride and accomplishment. As the meeting progressed, the team decided it was time to rework the performance goals based on what they have learned along the way. Based on these insights, a couple of people offer to drive the rework of the performance goals forward, with you to provide guidance. Though it was difficult for you to see their initial resistance, you feel energized by how the team is becoming self-sufficient.

Waiting to make the change and relying on past performance expectations was not the best move. Though your teams have been performing well, by the time performance reviews come, the goals no longer align to the work they are doing or the new business venture. Now, you need to meet with each team member individually to realign on expectations, which adds more to their plate.

Your decision to take these as on a case-by-case basis helps you in the short-term by avoiding introducing yet another change. Things continue to move forward smoothly, but overtime a few teams begin to fall behind as their performance expectations no longer align to the new business venture. As a result, you end up spending a lot of time meeting with team members individually to review their performance, reset expectations, and ease their concerns.

Your efforts to move quickly allow you to make progress and come up with suggested new performance expectations. However, you've missed out on the opportunity to involve the team and other leaders in the creation of these new goals, which would have created stronger adoption for the new approach. By leading the effort solo, you find that you not only face some resistance to the proposed changes, but also that they do not completely resonate with the realities of the work.

You have been with S&B Products for just over 5 years. You are a high performer who loves connecting with your colleagues, in addition to trying to keep up with the rapidly changing demands at S&B Products. In response to the transformation at S&B, your manager identified a new opportunity for you to join a project team that has, in the past, struggled to collaborate and work efficiently.

Your manager has shared that she feels your values and work ethic are aligned to the future of S&B Products and that you are in a great position to inspire and promote change. This is a pivotal leadership opportunity for you, but you have found yourself struggling with how to best help people on the team, while also staying true to the company vision and meeting the different deliverables for a successful product launch.

You have known Isaac since you first started with the organization; the two of you became close friends after sitting near each other during your first year at S&B. Isaac really values your trust and loyalty, so he feels comfortable sharing his feelings and frustrations with you. Isaac works on a different team, so although you are close personally, you have never had the chance to work together. You still find time to catch up on work and family life, usually over a cup of coffee every other Friday.

Isaac has been with S&B for 15 years and is highly respected in the organization. Lately, however, Isaac has been feeling distracted and has been complaining to you about how the new company initiatives are taking him away from his day-to-day work. Isaac values effective execution, efficiency, and completing his work within normal business hours. His risk-averse approach makes him cautious when confronted with anything new that might threaten his standard ways of working.

Lila is an ambitious mom who loves fashion, new restaurants, and exotic vacations. Lila was recruited heavily into the organization and has been a high performer since joining. You honestly do not know how she manages to do it all — between leading some of the most innovative projects at S&B Products, mothering two teenagers, and organizing working-mom events in the city…she continues to deliver and still manages to keep up with pace. Her busy schedule does make her a bit stressed at times and she has little patience for anything that takes too much time or explanation.

Some of your peers have mentioned that she has been having a hard time connecting with other's perspectives; she doesn't take the time to explain things and has high expectations. While she's a strong leader who's always on (it's amazing if she ever detaches from her cellphone) the way that she delivers feedback to her team is not always well-received. Yet, despite being unapproachable at times, Lila is great at making tough decisions and senior leadership trusts her opinion.

Ana is a recent new hire, and though she is a bit shy, she quickly became everyone's favorite person. She's friendly, easy to work with, and always delivers her work on time; however, she finds the pace of work much more stressful than her previous position in sales at another CPG company. Despite her bouncy positivity, you have noticed that whenever you ask her what her plans are outside of work, she tends to steer the conversation back to you.

Ana has always been task-oriented and detailed, but some people are starting to raise questions about her communication style and workload. Recently, a peer shared that Ana took on too much and missed an important deadline. In addition, you feel that she is not always clear on the company vision and feels a bit disconnected from the direction of the organization. Hugo, Ana's mentor, mentioned to you that in a recent meeting he was worried that she's nearing "burn out" with all that she is taking on. Given that she reports to you, this is something that you've been thinking about addressing with her — Ana makes everyone happy and has a lot of potential, so you want to see her thrive!

Hugo has to be one of the most creative minds in the organization. Hugo is passionate and loves to lead, yet he can sometimes be overbearing and does not always give enough space for other's ideas. His last big idea for an augmented reality application boosted sales and revolutionized the way S&B Products had previously used technology in product design. After this successful experience, he has become increasingly eager to contribute his ideas and come up with innovative products that will blow people away…again!

However, in the last six months, you have noticed that his ideas haven't been generating the excitement Hugo is hoping for. You know that Hugo was awarded a promotion after his "big augmented reality idea" and was given many new responsibilities (like mentoring Ana, the new hire). You can tell he has been working hard to get promoted again because he feels that working at S&B has really given him the opportunity to grow, but he is clearly feeling the pressure to uncover the next big thing.

You draft a note to send out to your BU. What do you communicate?

Despite some initial delays, the SLT is pleased with the work you're doing in your with the new venture. In fact, to support the venture, they've decided to acquire a competitor specializing in the type of new offerings your team has developed. Before you've settled on the news, you see a press release go out and the internal message boards lights up with chatter.

You see there is excitement within the company about the unique knowledge the acquired company will bring but you also see your teams are wondering if the work they have been doing could be in jeopardy. You sense some anxiety from your team about what this acquisition means for them and their roles. You have no doubt the acquisition will have significant impacts to your organization.

B. Stay Positive: You don't want your team's work to be overshadowed by the acquisition, but you trust senior leadership's decision. Your note says, "Good news!" and you tell your team, "Leadership recognizes our progress and is moving forward with our services by acquiring a new company to accelerate our strategy. I am not sure of the next steps, but as soon as I hear I will let you know."

D. Protect the Team: Relate to the frustrations with the acquisition, write, "I am so proud of the work we have all done. We've made significant progress on such a tight timeline. I will do my best to protect our work with the acquisition. While they bring new knowledge, they do not know our customers, our processes, or our culture as well as we do."

C. Be Transparent: You feel that the acquisition validates both the strategy and the work the team has done, but it is surprising that it happened so fast. Your note says, "The acquisition is great news as it will help us scale and give us the opportunity to learn from a company that used to be our biggest competitor. However, as is often the case in acquisitions, we do not yet know how this will come together. Regardless, we have always served our customer well and will continue to do so."

A. Create a New Plan: Acknowledge your team's concerns and write, "We invented this set of offerings for our company, and we have done an incredible job with the resources we have. The teams are reassured that you're on their side and keep working as normal. They will continue to turn to you for answers and trust that the promises you've made will be kept.

Your teams are disappointed with this decision, although they appreciate your optimism. Many come to you with questions you are not able to answer, while others simply disengage. Your teams' enthusiasm hits a new low and you are unsure what to do. This was an opportunity to be transparent about the challenges and opportunities the acquisition will bring and create a space for your teams to speak openly.

Being balanced in your reaction while emphasizing the opportunities and uncertainty with the acquisition is a smart move. Many of your team members are worried, but your approach of keeping the focus on how the business will serve the customers grounds a healthy conversation around their concerns and the potential opportunities which invites space for your people to speak openly. While some are still worried about their jobs, they are now more curious to understand more about the acquired business, their new peers, and what they themselves can do to create value going forward.

Your messaging sets the acquisition off on a wrong foot, positioning a culture of "us" vs. "them." Your teams share their frustrations and negative feelings towards this decision. Your teams are overwhelmed and uncertain about what will change, again, and you recognize how exhausted your teams must feel from the enormous effort they made earlier this year launching the new venture.

A. Strategic 1:1s: You see how demotivated the team is and convey this message to your boss. B. Time for Recognition: Schedule a meeting to let people share their concerns and frustrations. Talk to your direct reports to learn more about the business unit's morale. Plan special recognition and awards at the next BU meeting to congratulate them for their hard work and dedication to the company. You want your people to know how much you appreciate their resilience in times of change.

C. Stay Focused: Go to the leadership meeting to hear the updates and share them with your team and urge them to remember the company's priorities as we begin the integration. D. Collective Leadership: Schedule a meeting with your direct reports to discuss what you know and what you can share about the integration. Listen to their questions and admit that it is difficult to deal with the level of uncertainty. Together, brainstorm how to engage teams at all levels and how to reset priorities for their teams. With the broader BU, you recognize and acknowledge that people are exhausted. Identify the great, quality work they've accomplished, even in these uncertain times.

Your team appreciates your positivity, but it falls a bit flat. They are exhausted and do not see the purpose of all their work in the past few months without clarity on the acquisition. At this point, they are not sure how the integration will affect what they've been working so hard to accomplish. You missed an opportunity to have the tougher conversation and allow them to voice their perspective on how to best engage their teams moving forward.

Several teams are energized with the public recognition people needed. You notice, though, that several team members still seem skeptical and disengaged. There are doubts about the future, and rumors that some have started looking for job opportunities outside your company. Without clarity on what the integration will look like or being engaged in the conversation, it only creates further resistance to the change.

Your team feels like you are on their side, and this give them a lot of comfort. However, continuing to operate as normal only causes more frustration as there is a lack of clarity on priorities as well as how roles will change as the acquired company begins to integrate. This creates additional resistance to changes down the road as they don't see the need to deviate from normal operations.

Your collaborative approach yields some key insights. You learn that several teams are curious about how the company might develop with the integration. Through the brainstorming activity you identify ways people can balance the challenges of uncertainty with a focus on what they can control. While this can be a challenging conversation, leaning into it brings new perspectives and generates energy for the change.

Your team is adjusting to several changes at once. A new AI-enabled tool is being introduced to help improve speed and consistency in the work your team does. At the same time, processes are changing, priorities continue to shift, and teams are being asked to adapt while still meeting current service expectations.

Reactions on the team are mixed. Some teammates want to start using the tool broadly and move quickly. Others are more cautious — concerned about accuracy and maintaining quality. You are also hearing different messages: keep delivering, experiment with new ways of working, and stay flexible as guidance evolves. It is becoming harder to tell what the team should focus on first, and how quickly to change course.

Your team has been piloting a new way of working with a cross-functional team. The goal is to improve how work gets handed off between teams — catching issues earlier and reducing delays. It's a visible effort, and leadership is paying attention to how it goes.

The first real test didn't go well. The cross-functional team flagged concerns about the quality of what was shared, and now there's tension between the two groups. On your team, people are interpreting what happened very differently. Some feel the other team overreacted and created unnecessary friction. Others think your team moved too fast and didn't set the other team up well. A few aren't sure what to think, but they're picking up on the tension and it's affecting how they're showing up.

You've called a discussion to move things forward. But people are defending their interpretations rather than engaging with each other. There's pressure to respond to the other team.

A week after the team offsite, you are meeting with the senior leadership team. Although not fully bought in, you share the disruptive customer-first idea. Many of your peers on the leadership team agree it would disrupt more than our customer approach; it would require us to rethink parts of our strategy and change our ways of working.

Surprisingly, Maria, your boss, says she would like to hear more about what this could look like in action. You explain the idea and share the reasons it would be challenging to pursue, given other priorities. A few days later during a 1:1, Maria decides this is an area we need to explore. She suggests making it a customer-first initiative and ask you to lead the efforts.

"I'd like to see what you can make of this," Maria says. "Please come prepared with an update on your progress to present to the leadership team during our next meeting in two months."

During the past few weeks, there has been significant progress with the new customer-first initiative. You also notice that the initiative has brought prioritization and accountability challenges to your team. You have a plan to address these shifts with a new approach to some key roles in across Transformation Inc.

As you begin to communicate role changes with your peers on the leadership team, you discover that getting approvals for the new roles has been harder than you expected. Another week passes and you are still not able to announce the changes in roles and responsibilities.

To make matters worse, you learn that the other BU leaders did not expect the changes you proposed and believe it may cause challenges within their BUs which they don't have the bandwidth to address. Confusion and frustration are increasing throughout the organization and excitement for the initiative is dying down as ways of working are unable to keep pace with the changes. You decide to call sync with impacted leaders from your BU to explore the impact of the delays and share the future plan for the initiative.

It's been two months since you were asked to lead the efforts on a new customer-focused initiative, and you are ready to present your insights to the leadership team. At the meeting, you share some early positive customer feedback along with concerns that your continued focus on the initiative will cause your core business to suffer, especially as your teams are struggling with the various changes.

To your surprise, the leadership team is thrilled by the initial results with customers. As a result, they want to turn the initiative into a stand-alone business venture in support Transformation Inc.'s greater strategy. Your work on the initiative so far will be the foundation for the new venture, which will now become a significant part of your existing business. You must now decide how to best balance your obligations to the legacy business while supporting the launch of the new business.

The new venture is almost ready to launch, and you have been transparent about the big changes it will bring to your business unit. You notice a lot of different reactions to the changes from your people across your organization. Some are fully engaged and aligned, while others are doubtful and unsure about the implications the changes will have on their jobs.

In response to this feedback, you begin a reorganization to create new permanent teams, which unfortunately means you will break up teams who have worked well together.

As you begin to socialize the re-organization, the teams working on the legacy business feel demotivated because they feel their work is harder yet the value is increasingly unclear. At the same time, the teams transitioned to the new business feel uncertain about what they are supposed to do to succeed in this new role.

The new business venture is growing steadily, with more teams involved in supporting and developing its efforts. During a weekly meeting with one of your teams, they describe a challenge they are having with getting the commercial sales team to market the new offering. The current compensation plan for sales representatives promotes the legacy offerings over the new offerings.

You draft a recommendation for addressing this issue and meet with the compensation and commercial leads to present your thinking. Karim, the compensation lead, is skeptical, referring to the long and successful tenure of the existing structure. Further, Karim emphasizes an HR committee would need to make the approval for the change. Grace, the commercial lead, on the other hand, is more supportive, describing in detail her suggested next steps.

After hearing them both, you see merit in their concerns and ideas, but you have a different view on the best approach. The three of you leave the meeting with three divergent perspectives.

With the incentive structure now supporting the new venture and teams getting ready to launch, you sense some new confusion about the relationship between your business unit's strategy and the new venture's goals. After speaking with some team members to understand what people are confused about, you identify a gap in performance expectations and their goals.

Though you're hesitant to introduce more change, you recognize you need to update the business unit's performance expectations in the upcoming planning cycle. Some of your team members played a big role in creating the current plan and you bring a suggested change to performance expectations to them first.

Their response was resoundingly negative. Most of your team feels there is no need to change the current goals or expectations.
